critic_raw:
Assessor A: The candidate generator improvement is measured in Recall@50. The business outcome is watch time. Between these lies a multi-stage pipeline with a ranker, satisfaction guardrails, repetition filters, and maturity filters. The paper does not analyze what fraction of the additionally recalled candidates survive this pipeline to influence the home page. Without this analysis, Recall@50 is an upper bound on improvement, not an estimate. Verdict: critique_wins.

Assessor B: The offline-online gap in recommendation systems is one of the most extensively documented problems in the field. A 17% Recall@50 improvement is a good development result but does not justify skipping online holdback for a costly migration. If the offline metric were highly correlated with watch time for this system, that correlation analysis should be presented. It is not. Verdict: critique_wins.

Assessor C: The paper correctly notes it has no online experiment and wants to use offline results to justify migration. This logic fails because (1) the metric is an upstream proxy for the actual outcome, (2) the offline-online gap is uncharacterized, and (3) the downstream pipeline may filter the improvement entirely. Verdict: critique_wins.

Synthesis: Unanimous critique_wins across three assessors. All three identify the same three issues.
adjudication_raw: Unanimous critique_wins. All three assessors identify upstream proxy metric, offline-online gap, and downstream survival as the three core issues. No ETD — the critique is structural for the purpose of justifying migration without online validation.
